EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Saturated soils from the previous rainfall events enhanced the risk of flash and river flooding, as another mid to upper-level trough lingered across the region. With increased instability across the Northeast Caribbean, and as a surface high pressure migrated from the western to central Atlantic, moisture was pooled into the region, with total precipitable water values well above the climatological value for these dates. Most of the USGS monitored rivers showed above normal to high levels.|Each day the amount of rainfall was locally significant. In fact, rescues were needed in San Sebasti��n, and Yauco.  The rivers went out of their banks in A��asco, Utuado, Orocovis, Vega Baja, Corozal, Arecibo, San Germ��n, Hormigueros, Mayag��ez, San Juan, Ponce, Ciales, Manat��, and many others without a river gage. At the end of the event, a significant portion of the interior and western Puerto Rico collected over 10���, with isolated amounts surpassing 14. Areas in the east collected more than 5���, all of this in only a couple of days. An astonishing amount of 32 flash flood warnings were issued during this short period of time, with 115 Local Storm Reports created as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A landslide was reported at PR-155, Ramal 567, near Hacienda El Patrimonio. The road was reported as impassable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Locally generated rip currents on a low-risk day caused a drowning in Isabela.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tourist drowned on Jobos Beach.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Remnants of a cold front and light steering winds generated slow-moving showers around Toa Baja and its vicinity, which led to flooding in Levitown late in the night. Also, a funnel cloud developed earlier in the day in Culebra.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency managers reported several impassable roads in the 3rd section of Levittown, including Paseo Conde, Paseo Criolla, and Paseo Criolla.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Remnants of a cold front and light steering winds generated slow-moving showers around Toa Baja and its vicinity, which led to flooding in Levitown late in the night. Also, a funnel cloud developed earlier in the day in Culebra.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported through social media (Facebook) a funnel cloud just east of Zoni Beach in Culebra.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The day started sunny on the islands as a cold front approached from the northwest. Instability was enhanced, and the moisture associated with the front triggered scattered to numerous showers across most of Puerto Rico. Later in the day, as the frontal boundary crossed the region, showers became widespread and strong, with thunderstorm activity detected. Rainfall accumulations ranged from 2 to 5 inches over the eastern interior and just southwest of the San Juan metro area. A few rivers over north-central Puerto Rico reached flood stage, and flash flood warnings were issued. Reports due to small streams out of their banks flooding small bridges and roads were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was occurring at PR-160 in direction toward Pueblo Nuevo since Rio Cibuco was out of it banks. A car was reported trapped in this flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low level moisture was converging across Puerto Rico under an east to east-northeast wind flow. A line of persistent showers developed along the north-central coast, leaving rainfall accumulations of 1 to 2 inches in some areas. The next day, a mudslide was reported in the affected areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A small landslide was reported near road PR-2 in Arecibo, close to the funeral home Cristo Rey.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very stable weather conditions were prevailing in Puerto Rico, with a dry air mass in place. Moisture levels were significantly lower than climatological values. The winds were strong, at 15 mph or more, and relative humidities were anticipated to drop to 55% or less. With these drier conditions, the risk for the spreading of fires was elevated. The smoke cloud from a grassfire that developed in the southwest reached two people, that required medical assistance.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A forest fire that developed in San German consumed near 4 acres of terrain. Two individuals were affected by smoke inhalation and were transported to the hospital.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low-pressure system located approximately 900 miles north of Puerto Rico generated significant northerly swells during the week of March 27th. These swells caused high surf and life-threatening rip currents along the Atlantic coastline of the islands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 40-year-old male tourist and his two daughters were swept away by rip currents at Montones Beach in Isabela. The man managed to rescue the girls but was also dragged and unable to get out of the water. Paramedics from State Medical Emergencies provided first aid and confirmed that the individual no longer had vital signs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Wind-driven seas and a fading long-period northeasterly swell caused a high risk of rip currents along Puerto Rico's Atlantic Coastline.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A family of seven was swept away by rip currents in Playa Escondida, Fajardo. The father was able to save all his children and wife, but he drowned, and his body was recovered two days later in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Seas were slowly building across the Atlantic waters on Sunday, March 10th, from swells generated by a distant surface low. Although this would lead to a high risk of rip currents on Monday, a moderate risk prevailed through Sunday.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two males were swept away by rip currents near Ventana del Mar, behind La Concha Hotel in Condado. According to initial reports, a local resident tried to rescue a tourist who had been pulled away by the currents. They both drowned. The first victim was identified as a 20-year-old male from Washington visiting the island for Spring Break. The second victim was a staff sergeant in the Puerto Rico Army National Guard, his body was recovered three miles from the beach two days later on March 12.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Large northerly swells affected the Atlantic coastline of Puerto Rico during the week of March 13th. This produced large breaking waves higher than 10 feet and life-threatening rip currents along the north and east-facing beaches of the islands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A rip current swept away a 52-year-old male tourist from Minnesota at La Pared Beach in Luquillo on March 13th. The person was rescued and taken to the Caribbean Medical Center in critical condition, where he died.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low-pressure system located approximately 900 miles north of Puerto Rico generated significant northerly swells during the week of March 27th. These swells caused high surf and life-threatening rip currents along the Atlantic coastline of the islands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A rescue operation began on March 28 for a 26-year-old tourist, an active duty U.S. marine, and a resident of Massachusetts who was swept away by rip currents on La Pared beach in Luquillo on March 27. The Coast Guard rescue crews suspended search efforts on April 1 in support of local emergency response agencies. He is presumed dead.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep-layered trough along with several frontal boundaries was approaching the Caribbean Islands in the first week of February. The winds became light and from the south-southwest, pulling plenty of moisture from the Caribbean Sea. First, several clusters of showers moved across western Puerto Rico, but the rain then spread eastward during the day. In the evening hours, the rain reached the Virgin Islands, with pulses of heavy rain observed. The next day was wet as well, although not as intense, with several periods of continuous light to moderate showers. In Puerto Rico, the areas around Caguas collected up to 5 inches, while 3 to 5 inches fell in general in the east, interior and west. A few rivers reached flood stage with this event, but the main impacts were associated with strong gusty winds that resulted in reports of several tree limbs in the ground.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was reported at Calle Montreal, close to Joseph Barber.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep-layered trough along with several frontal boundaries was approaching the Caribbean Islands in the first week of February. The winds became light and from the south-southwest, pulling plenty of moisture from the Caribbean Sea. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Beryl moved south of Puerto Rico early in the month, as a powerful category five storm. The system brought breezy to windy conditions to the islands, as well as passing showers and thunderstorms. The damage associated with these winds was not widespread, but some gusty winds managed to blow a couple of tents away, as well as to knock down a tree. The major impact associated with this hurricane, however, was the deterioration of the coastal conditions, with several reports of sea water and sand reaching coastal roads. The roads had to be closed and cleaned afterward.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At least a dozen tents were damaged due to strong wind gusts at PR-305, km 2.2 in Lajas. These tents were part of an Agricultural Festival that was to be held there from July 4th to July 7th. Outer rainbands from hurricane Beryl reached the area promoting squally weather during the morning of July 2nd. The damage to the tents and saturated soils due to rainfall forced the managers to start the festival on the 5th.

This caused seas to increase between 4 and 5 feet across the southern coastal waters of Puerto Rico, and a moderate risk of rip currents was expected across the south to southeastern beaches of Puerto Rico. One drowning occurred in Guilarte Beach in Arroyo.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The moisture field of a tropical wave that was approaching the islands from the southeast and a low-level jet promoted wet and unstable conditions. Before midday, activity intensified and cloudiness increased resulting in an active day with multiple flood advisories and flash flood warnings due to a strong severe thunderstorm that|moved into the southeast bringing periods of heavy rain and wind gusts. In Guayama and Yabucoa, wind gusts of 50 and 48 mph respectively were recorded. Municipalities of the eastern and southeastern quadrants of Puerto Rico received the greatest accumulations from 3 to 5.5 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency managers reported flood waters from Rio Blanco affecting roads PR-31 and PR-191. Both roads were impassable. A USGS river gauge upstream reported a max height of around 8.5 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Puerto Rico observed two events with above-normal moisture and instability from May 3rd through the 9th. The weather scenario for May 3rd involved an upper-level trough and an induced surface trough with deep moisture from the Caribbean, which enhanced thunderstorms and downpours across most of PR. Besides those atmospheric conditions, it is essential to highlight that April ended up being one of the wettest periods for any other end of the month since we have historical records. The USGS river streamflow stations reported that most of the eastern half of the rivers had streamflows from above-normal to much above-normal ahead of the event. This situation triggered a Flood Watch that was issued for the islands from Friday through Sunday.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was reported at PR-167 at the stop light in front of Plaza del Parque. A car was trapped in the flooding, with people inside.

The USGS river streamflow stations reported that most of the eastern half of the rivers had streamflows from above-normal to much above-normal ahead of the event. This situation triggered a Flood Watch that was issued for the islands from Friday through Sunday.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A small mudslide was reported in Calle Betania in Trujillo Alto.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The proximity of an upper level trough, along with an area of enhanced moisture, very light winds and strong heating resulted in explosive convection developing over Puerto Rico. The thunderstorms produced very heavy rainfall, lightning, gusty winds, and even hail. Flooding and landslides were reported in several areas in Puerto Rico. Water surges along rivers were common, with some rivers even reaching flood stage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A social media post reported that PR-497 by El Yugo was impassable due to Rio Sonador being out of its banks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unsettled weather conditions prevailed across Puerto Rico through the first week of September as a retrograding upper level trough lingered in the region. The instability of the trough, diurnal heating, and low level moisture combined to generate widespread showers and thunderstorms across the region. Each day, accumulations ranged from 2 to 3 inches, with maximum amounts up to 5 inches in some areas. This led to several reports of mudslides, lightning, and gusty winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck the roof of a house in Residencial Fray Bartolome de las Casas in Santurce. Damage to the roof was visible from inside the house, with paint surrounding the area stripped away and small debris visible on the living room. The house was left without electrical power. Paramedics visited the scene, but everyone was ok.

A mid to upper-level trough remained close enough to the region to generate shower and thunderstorms activity. Conditions gradually improved across the region, but enough instability remained, and showers and thunderstorms developed each afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two cars were impacted by a branch that fell from a tree in front of Cobian Plaza, Ave. Ponce de Leon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southeasterly winds steering warmer tropical air into the islands, and sufficient moisture lead to heat index values exceeding Heat Advisory criteria across most coastal and lower elevation areas. Heat index values exceeded Excessive Heat Warning criteria on up to several days of the second half of May across San Juan and Vicinity, North Central Puerto Rico and Northwestern PR. Several stations across those areas reported heat indices surpassing 112 degrees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several weather stations reported heat indices above 112 degrees for up to over two hours. These conditions were observed widespread along the coastal areas and the valleys.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unsettled weather conditions prevailed across Puerto Rico through the first week of September as a retrograding upper level trough lingered in the region. The instability of the trough, diurnal heating, and low level moisture combined to generate widespread showers and thunderstorms across the region. Each day, accumulations ranged from 2 to 3 inches, with maximum amounts up to 5 inches in some areas. This led to several reports of mudslides, lightning, and gusty winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck the outside wall of a house in Urbanizacion Mirador del Sol in Cabo Rojo. Damage was visible from outside the house, with paint and some cement surrounding the areas (entry and exit points were visible and showed damage) stripped away. Small debris was visible in the garden and driveway.

This led to several reports of mudslides, lightning, and flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public reported on social media that Road 31 in Naguabo is being affected by flooding due to Rio Blanco overflowing since around 1 to 2.30 inches have fallen.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After the previous rainfall events, soils remained saturated across the region, maintaining the risk of flooding at significant levels. A mid to upper-level trough remained close enough to the region to generate shower and thunderstorms activity. Conditions gradually improved across the region, but enough instability remained, and showers and thunderstorms developed each afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was reported around American Gasoline, PR-459 and PR-467.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southeasterly winds steering warmer tropical air into the islands, and sufficient moisture lead to heat index values exceeding Heat Advisory criteria across most coastal and lower elevation areas. Heat index values exceeded Excessive Heat Warning criteria on up to several days of the second half of May across San Juan and Vicinity, North Central Puerto Rico and Northwestern PR. Several stations across those areas reported heat indices surpassing 112 degrees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several weather stations reported heat indices above 112 degrees for up to over two hours. These conditions were observed widespread along the coastal areas and the valleys.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The proximity of an upper level trough, along with an area of enhanced moisture, very light winds and strong heating resulted in explosive convection developing over Puerto Rico. The thunderstorms produced very heavy rainfall, lightning, gusty winds, and even hail. Flooding and landslides were reported in several areas in Puerto Rico. Water surges along rivers were common, with some rivers even reaching flood stage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency managers reported a bridge in PR-167 by Sierra Linda completely flooded due to the river being out of its banks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak high pressure is ridding over the region from a high pressure just west of the Canary Islands maintained light and variable winds. This promoted seas of 3 to 4 feet, occasionally higher over the Atlantic waters of Puerto Rico. There was a moderate risk of rip currents in effect for the northern beaches of Puerto Rico, including Isabela. One rip current related drowning occurred in Jobos Beach.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A newspaper reported that a 59-year-old tourist was spending time with his 9-year-old son and 12-year-old daughter at Jobos Beach when they were swept away by rip currents. Two surfers at the beach rescued the children and then returned to retrieve the father, whom they brought to shore, but he had no vital signs upon arrival.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface high pressure across the Atlantic promoted gentle to moderate easterly winds. Seas were around 4 feet, occasionally to 5 feet, over the coastal northern waters of Puerto Rico. There was a moderate risk of rip currents in effect for the northern beaches of Puerto Rico, including San Juan. One rip current related drowning occurred in Condado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 22-year-old woman from Indianapolis, USA, drowned at Condado Beach in San Juan while attempting to rescue two people who had been swept away by a rip current. The incident occurred at approximately 7:00 p.m. on September 8th. Although the two people she was trying to save were rescued, she unfortunately died, and her body was recovered on the afternoon of September 9th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Puerto Rico observed two events with above-normal moisture and instability from May 3rd through the 9th. The weather scenario for May 3rd involved an upper-level trough and an induced surface trough with deep moisture from the Caribbean, which enhanced thunderstorms and downpours across most of PR. Besides those atmospheric conditions, it is essential to highlight that April ended up being one of the wettest periods for any other end of the month since we have historical records. The USGS river streamflow stations reported that most of the eastern half of the rivers had streamflows from above-normal to much above-normal ahead of the event. This situation triggered a Flood Watch that was issued for the islands from Friday through Sunday.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was reported at Calle El Monte, intersection with Calle Fernando Arrivi in Barrio Campanilla.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The interaction of Hurricane Kirk and another low pressure across the north Atlantic modulated the local weather pattern, pooling abundant moisture and promoting light winds in the region. Additionally, increasing instability due to the proximity of a mid to upper level trough favored deeper convection development each afternoon. This resulted in widespread showers and thunderstorms each day along Puerto Rico, with daily amounts ranging from 2 to 4 inches, and localized higher amounts. With an elevated flooding risk, several flood and landslide reports were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency Manager Director from Ponce reported flooding at Santiago de Los Caballeros Avenue in front of the Departmento del Trabajo Building near La Guancha.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of cold fronts and a polar trough increased moisture and instability across the region for the last three days of October. Moisture levels went well above the climatological value, leading to a significant increase in showers and thunderstorms. Showers were very numerous, with rainfall accumulations surpassing 8 inches in sectors of northeastern and southeastern Puerto Rico. Soils were saturated, and rivers were running very high, triggering the issuance of a Flood Watch. With the excessive rainfall, multiple flash flooding events and mudslides unfolded.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was reported at PR-8860, near Urbanization Bosque de Santa Maria.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prefrontal trough, an approaching tropical wave, and the remnants of Tropical Storm Oscar combined to generate showers and thunderstorms across the area. The diurnal heating and the available moisture from these features also contributed to the development of strong convection across the interior and western Puerto Rico each afternoon. In general, 2 to 3 inches of rainfall were registered each day. This led to urban flooding, as well as mudslides in several municipalities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flood waters from Rio Blanco were observed on Road PR-31 in sector La Pitina, Naguabo.  The road was impassable. Two people were also trapped inside the river, sheltering over a rock. The individuals were rescued.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The islands of Puerto Rico were under a Tropical Storm Warning due to the tropical cyclone threats associated with Ernesto. Ernesto approached the islands late Tuesday night into Wednesday as a tropical storm intensified into a hurricane across the Atlantic Ocean north of the islands on the 14th at around 11 AM AST. Before the event unfolded, soils were saturated along many of western Puerto Rico, the San Juan metropolitan area, the southeast, and Vieques and Culebra. The 30-day rainfall surplus was 4 to 8 inches above normal before Ernesto. Additionally, many streams were running normal or above normal. ||The first rainbands associated with the tropical storm arrived on Tuesday under a northeasterly wind flow. During the night hours of Tuesday, the first strong area of rainfall approached eastern Puerto Rico. The system quickly dumped over four inches of rainfall across Vieques and Culebra. The rain then reached eastern Puerto Rico, with areas around Maunabo collecting over three inches in a short period of time. This rainfall triggered flash flooding, and rivers quickly reacted. ||Strong gusty winds were observed as the hurricane's outer rainbands moved over the islands. In Puerto Rico, the strongest gust was 86 mph on Isla Culebrita, Culebra. The Roosevelt Road weather station also reported sustained tropical storm force winds, with a 74-mph gust, marking the 8th strong reading at this station. Reports included fallen trees, downed power lines, and some roof damage. ||Ernesto brought significant flooding, along with tropical storm-force sustained winds and hurricane-force wind gusts, which caused substantial damage across Puerto Rico. More than 125 Local Storm Reports have been generated. Nearly 65% were associated with wind gust damage and tropical cyclone conditions, while 32% were associated with flooding and landslides. The power grid was affected by the tropical-force winds and heavy rains. The agricultors estimated millions of dollars in losses. The initial estimates for the damages caused by Ernesto are approximately 5 million dollars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree obstructing PR-391 at Barrio Tallaboa Alta, Las Lavanderas sector in Penuelas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Locally induced afternoon thunderstorms over western Puerto Rico led to rockfall and mudslides.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An emergency manager from OMME San Sebastian reported a rockfall at PR-111 Km 23.9, close to Salto Collazo in Barrio Piedras Blancas. Social media posts show a 2013 Kia Rio severely impacted by the rockfall. However, the car driver was not harmed.

The weather scenario for May 3rd involved an upper-level trough and an induced surface trough with deep moisture from the Caribbean, which enhanced thunderstorms and downpours across most of PR. Besides those atmospheric conditions, it is essential to highlight that April ended up being one of the wettest periods for any other end of the month since we have historical records. The USGS river streamflow stations reported that most of the eastern half of the rivers had streamflows from above-normal to much above-normal ahead of the event. This situation triggered a Flood Watch that was issued for the islands from Friday through Sunday.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency managers reported flooding affecting a house in Calle 8, Reparto Marquez.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of cold fronts and a polar trough increased moisture and instability across the region for the last three days of October. Moisture levels went well above the climatological value, leading to a significant increase in showers and thunderstorms. Showers were very numerous, with rainfall accumulations surpassing 8 inches in sectors of northeastern and southeastern Puerto Rico. Soils were saturated, and rivers were running very high, triggering the issuance of a Flood Watch. With the excessive rainfall, multiple flash flooding events and mudslides unfolded.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency managers reported flash flooding (urban) affecting portions of Calle Dufresne and Calle Antonio Lopez, near the Obelisk in tribute to the Humacao Teachers and the Municipal Police offices. These same flood waters affected a nearby elementary school. These roads were impassable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tropical wave crossed the islands during the beginning of August, from Thursday, August 1st, through Saturday, August 3rd, which resulted in an unstable weather pattern. This weather scenario was enhanced by local topography, sea breeze variations, and afternoon effects typically observed in Puerto Rico, resulting in flooding and strong gusty winds due to thunderstorm activity.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Due to strong thunderstorms, Moca's emergency management reported a fallen tree on Road 444, near the baseball field, close to Carretera Nieves.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tropical wave crossed the islands during the beginning of August, from Thursday, August 1st, through Saturday, August 3rd, which resulted in an unstable weather pattern. This weather scenario was enhanced by local topography, sea breeze variations, and afternoon effects typically observed in Puerto Rico, resulting in flooding and strong gusty winds due to thunderstorm activity.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms developed in the northwest quadrant, causing flooding in the western Municipalities. A social media report showed a river flooding along road PR-125, kilometer 10.1, in Barrio Capa, Moca, towards Anasco.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tropical wave crossed the islands during the beginning of August, from Thursday, August 1st, through Saturday, August 3rd, which resulted in an unstable weather pattern. This weather scenario was enhanced by local topography, sea breeze variations, and afternoon effects typically observed in Puerto Rico, resulting in flooding and strong gusty winds due to thunderstorm activity.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding rains caused by strong thunderstorms affected the northwest Municipalities. A social media post indicated minor flooding related to a water surge along a river that crossed a road near Barrio Saltos in San Sebastian, near a residential area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Above-normal moisture associated with a tropical wave combined with increased instability due to an upper-level trough and enhanced by local effects and a sea breeze promoted thunderstorm formation across PR's interior and western sections, triggering flooding and mudslides.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The residents along Calle Theopolis in Mayaguez shared with a TV meteorologist (which was then confirmed with Mayaguez's Emergency Managers) that flooding was affecting their neighborhood. The Doppler radar estimated between 2 and 3 inches of rain due to strong thunderstorms, and the runoff from those rains was the leading cause of the flooding problem observed, mainly along the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Above-normal moisture associated with a tropical wave combined with increased instability due to an upper-level trough and enhanced by local effects and a sea breeze promoted thunderstorm formation across PR's interior and western sections, triggering flooding and mudslides.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The residents along road Alheli at Urb Buenaventura in Mayaguez shared with a TV meteorologist (which was then confirmed with Mayaguez's Emergency Managers) that flooding was affecting their neighborhood due to poor drainage near a clogged sewer. The Doppler radar estimated between 2 and 3 inches of rain due to strong thunderstorms, which was the leading cause of the flooding problem observed, mainly along the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Above-normal moisture associated with a tropical wave combined with increased instability due to an upper-level trough and enhanced by local effects and a sea breeze promoted thunderstorm formation across PR's interior and western sections, triggering flooding and mudslides.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong thunderstorm produced heavy rains that triggered a mudslide along road PR-105 in Sector Margarita, Barrio Lim��n, at Mayaguez, making it impassable���the Doppler radar rainfall estimated around 2 inches of rain during the event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of hot temperatures, high low-level moisture, and a Saharan Air Layer results in dangerous heat levels for people's health, especially in urban and coastal areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station GW0592 Moca reported 115F, and the heat index remained above 112F for more than 2 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The area was experiencing an east-to-southeasterly wind pattern with above-normal sea surface temperatures, warmer-than-normal minimum temperatures, and near-to-above-normal moisture content. This pattern led to higher-than-average temperatures throughout the end of August despite the arrival of weak tropical waves. As a result, the urban and coastal areas of Puerto Rico experienced a heat spell, prompting the issuance of Heat Advisories and Excessive Heat Warnings during this period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Stations in Ponce registered heat indices of 112 degrees or more for two hours or more each day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The islands of Puerto Rico were under a Tropical Storm Warning due to the tropical cyclone threats associated with Ernesto. Ernesto approached the islands late Tuesday night into Wednesday as a tropical storm intensified into a hurricane across the Atlantic Ocean north of the islands on the 14th at around 11 AM AST. Before the event unfolded, soils were saturated along many of western Puerto Rico, the San Juan metropolitan area, the southeast, and Vieques and Culebra. The 30-day rainfall surplus was 4 to 8 inches above normal before Ernesto. Additionally, many streams were running normal or above normal. ||The first rainbands associated with the tropical storm arrived on Tuesday under a northeasterly wind flow. During the night hours of Tuesday, the first strong area of rainfall approached eastern Puerto Rico. The system quickly dumped over four inches of rainfall across Vieques and Culebra. The rain then reached eastern Puerto Rico, with areas around Maunabo collecting over three inches in a short period of time. This rainfall triggered flash flooding, and rivers quickly reacted. ||Strong gusty winds were observed as the hurricane's outer rainbands moved over the islands. In Puerto Rico, the strongest gust was 86 mph on Isla Culebrita, Culebra. The Roosevelt Road weather station also reported sustained tropical storm force winds, with a 74-mph gust, marking the 8th strong reading at this station. Reports included fallen trees, downed power lines, and some roof damage. ||Ernesto brought significant flooding, along with tropical storm-force sustained winds and hurricane-force wind gusts, which caused substantial damage across Puerto Rico. More than 125 Local Storm Reports have been generated. Nearly 65% were associated with wind gust damage and tropical cyclone conditions, while 32% were associated with flooding and landslides. The power grid was affected by the tropical-force winds and heavy rains. The agricultors estimated millions of dollars in losses. The initial estimates for the damages caused by Ernesto are approximately 5 million dollars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding affected a house on PR-921 in Humacao.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The islands of Puerto Rico were under a Tropical Storm Warning due to the tropical cyclone threats associated with Ernesto. Ernesto approached the islands late Tuesday night into Wednesday as a tropical storm intensified into a hurricane across the Atlantic Ocean north of the islands on the 14th at around 11 AM AST. Before the event unfolded, soils were saturated along many of western Puerto Rico, the San Juan metropolitan area, the southeast, and Vieques and Culebra. The 30-day rainfall surplus was 4 to 8 inches above normal before Ernesto. Additionally, many streams were running normal or above normal. ||The first rainbands associated with the tropical storm arrived on Tuesday under a northeasterly wind flow. During the night hours of Tuesday, the first strong area of rainfall approached eastern Puerto Rico. The system quickly dumped over four inches of rainfall across Vieques and Culebra. The rain then reached eastern Puerto Rico, with areas around Maunabo collecting over three inches in a short period of time. This rainfall triggered flash flooding, and rivers quickly reacted. ||Strong gusty winds were observed as the hurricane's outer rainbands moved over the islands. In Puerto Rico, the strongest gust was 86 mph on Isla Culebrita, Culebra. The Roosevelt Road weather station also reported sustained tropical storm force winds, with a 74-mph gust, marking the 8th strong reading at this station. Reports included fallen trees, downed power lines, and some roof damage. ||Ernesto brought significant flooding, along with tropical storm-force sustained winds and hurricane-force wind gusts, which caused substantial damage across Puerto Rico. More than 125 Local Storm Reports have been generated. Nearly 65% were associated with wind gust damage and tropical cyclone conditions, while 32% were associated with flooding and landslides. The power grid was affected by the tropical-force winds and heavy rains. The agricultors estimated millions of dollars in losses. The initial estimates for the damages caused by Ernesto are approximately 5 million dollars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The 911 Call Center received a report of floodwaters across PR-805 near Hacienda Maria. The floodwaters were entering a house, and two people were trapped inside.

In Puerto Rico, the strongest gust was 86 mph on Isla Culebrita, Culebra. Reports included fallen trees, downed power lines, and some roof damage. ||Ernesto brought significant flooding, along with tropical storm-force sustained winds and hurricane-force wind gusts, which caused substantial damage across Puerto Rico. Ernesto approached the islands late Tuesday night into Wednesday as a tropical storm intensified into a hurricane across the Atlantic Ocean north of the islands on the 14th at around 11 AM AST. Before the event unfolded, soils were saturated along many of western Puerto Rico, the San Juan metropolitan area, the southeast, and Vieques and Culebra. The 30-day rainfall surplus was 4 to 8 inches above normal before Ernesto. Additionally, many streams were running normal or above normal. ||The first rainbands associated with the tropical storm arrived on Tuesday under a northeasterly wind flow. During the night hours of Tuesday, the first strong area of rainfall approached eastern Puerto Rico. The system quickly dumped over four inches of rainfall across Vieques and Culebra. The rain then reached eastern Puerto Rico, with areas around Maunabo collecting over three inches in a short period of time. This rainfall triggered flash flooding, and rivers quickly reacted. ||Strong gusty winds were observed as the hurricane's outer rainbands moved over the islands. In Puerto Rico, the strongest gust was 86 mph on Isla Culebrita, Culebra. The Roosevelt Road weather station also reported sustained tropical storm force winds, with a 74-mph gust, marking the 8th strong reading at this station. Reports included fallen trees, downed power lines, and some roof damage. ||Ernesto brought significant flooding, along with tropical storm-force sustained winds and hurricane-force wind gusts, which caused substantial damage across Puerto Rico. More than 125 Local Storm Reports have been generated. Nearly 65% were associated with wind gust damage and tropical cyclone conditions, while 32% were associated with flooding and landslides. The power grid was affected by the tropical-force winds and heavy rains. The agricultors estimated millions of dollars in losses. The initial estimates for the damages caused by Ernesto are approximately 5 million dollars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media reported a fallen tree affecting PR-8860 in Trujillo Alto. Estimated wind gust from nearby station at the time of event, radar estimated 35 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Pulses of northerly swells moving across the local waters, along with fresh to locally strong easterly winds, promoted hazardous coastal conditions for the local islands. Exposed coastal areas, including Culebra, had a High Risk of Rip Currents due to breaking waves between 6 to 8 feet. A rip current related death occurred in Culebra.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 68-year-old man from Connecticut, a resident of Culebra, was last seen being dragged by a rip current at Flamenco Beach, Culebra. His body was found after two days.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On Christmas Day, a high risk of rip currents was in effect for northern Puerto Rico until 6 PM AST (becoming moderate after) due to increased winds and breaking waves around 6 feet. Unfortunately, under these hazardous coastal conditions a rip current related fatality was reported in Luquillo.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 90-year-old resident in Luquillo and a visitor from Utah, USA, was found dead, likely due to drowning, approximately 30 miles offshore from Costa Azul in Luquillo. The incident occurred around 5 PM AST on Christmas Day, and Emergency Managers initiated the search and rescue mission with the US Coast Guard after he was reported missing at 9:30 PM. According to reports from Luquillo's Emergency Management director and the US Coast Guard, it is believed that he died after being caught in a rip current that dragged him out to sea.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of an old frontal boundary approached and then crossed the islands on the 28th and 29th as a strong surface high pressure ridge that moved eastward. This pushed bands of low level moisture and brought periods of showers to the northern half of Puerto Rico. During the morning of the 28th, a landslide was reported near the Dona Juana Waterfall in Orocovis. During the 24 hours before the event, nearby stations in Matrullas Lake and Guineo Lake, reported 1.16 and 0.93 in, respectively. Since Christmas Eve, those stations had 1.57 and 1.07 in, respectively.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A landslide was reported at PR-149 in Orocovis, near KM 40.5, the closed business La Bodega de Las Brujas, and the Dona Juana Waterfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long period northerly swell created hazardous coastal conditions along the north-facing beaches of PR and the US Virgin Islands during the third weekend of November. The exposed Atlantic coastline were  impacted by breaking waves between 8 and 15 feet. Additionally, the full moon phase exacerbated conditions, with isolated coastal flooding along the most vulnerable beaches of northern PR. Coastal Flood Advisories, High Surf Advisories and High Risk of Rip Currents were in effect. One rip current related drowning occurred in Arecibo.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 40-year-old male tourist from Michigan drowned at La Cueva del Indio, near road PR-681 in the Islote sector of Arecibo. According to reports, he was caught in a rip        current.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Flash Flood Watch was issued on the second weekend of November, mainly due to a tropical wave moving through the area and a lingering frontal boundary. Saturated soils and moderate to heavy rainfall helped increase the flood and debris flow threat. This tropical wave brought abundant moisture above typical November levels, along with instability, leading to thunderstorms with frequent lightning, strong, gusty winds, and breezy to windy conditions in parts of the islands. From November 9th to 11th, above 3 inches of rain were reported, across SW and N Puerto Rico, but mainly over SE Puerto Rico and Vieques, (with some stations reporting 5 to 6.69 inches in those areas).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: PR-987, KM 4.8, in Bo. Las Croabas was impassable due to flooding. The road was closed by officials during the early morning hours.

The home showed badly damaged walls and columns, as well as a sinkhole in the floor. Emergency managers reported that the home and surrounding land are 5 feet deep from before the landslide.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: By the 16th, moisture remained from a front that passed Puerto Rico and the U.S. |Virgin Islands, and was located southeast of them. A second front was approaching the area and brought additional rain late on the 16th and the 17th. In addition a surface trough and an upper level trough also helped increased flooding risk, and promoted unsettled weather conditions that will lead to flooding rains in several location across the area. Stations over southeast PR (the most affected) reported rainfall between 3-6 inches over that weekend. Several flooding and flash flooding events were reported, including one that sadly led to a fatality.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was reported at the intersection of PR-3 and Dr. Vidal St. Flooding reached the parking of several businesses in this intersection, including Del Este Supermarket and Martins BBQ. Humacao River is also near this area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around 8:44 PM on November 16, 2024, a 63-year-old male and his passenger, a 39-year-old woman, crossed a low-level crossing/bridge in Barrio Guayabota, Sector Sasa, particularly on road 182 km 12. This bridge was covered with water as Guayanes River went over it due to the heavy rainfall activity that the municipality of Yabucoa was receiving. Radar estimates and observations suggested around 2 to 4 inches of rain in the area of the incident and around 6 inches across the municipality. As they crossed the bridge, the Ford F-150 that they were in got dragged into the fast-flowing river. During this event, the woman was trapped in the vehicle, that became lodged under rubble, and drowned.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unsettled and variable weather conditions persisted on Nov 23rd as moisture from the Caribbean moved across the region, with its related showers, and a deep polar trough deepened across the northwestern Caribbean. This maintained an elevated risk of flooding in some areas. A flood advisory was issued for several municipalities, including Manati, at around 623 PM AST.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A stranded vehicle was reported in PR-2 in Manati near Villa Maria Shopping Center.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of cold fronts and a polar trough increased moisture and instability across the region to end October and start November. Above normal moisture, saturated soils, and rivers running high increased the risk of flooding. A Flash Flood Watch was in effect on November 1st with Debris Flow, Sinkholes, Flooding and Flash Flooding being reported in southeastern and north-central Puerto Rico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Honda Creek out of its banks flooded PR-31, KM14.9, leaving both lanes impassable. This is near Edgardo Auto Parts and the intersection with PR-936.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of cold fronts and a polar trough increased moisture and instability across the region to end October and start November. Above normal moisture, saturated soils, and rivers running high increased the risk of flooding. A Flash Flood Watch was in effect on November 1st with Debris Flow, Sinkholes, Flooding and Flash Flooding being reported in southeastern and north-central Puerto Rico.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: By the 16th, moisture remained from a front that passed Puerto Rico and the U.S. |Virgin Islands, and was located southeast of them. A second front was approaching the area and brought additional rain late on the 16th and the 17th. In addition a surface trough and an upper level trough also helped increased flooding risk, and promoted unsettled weather conditions that will lead to flooding rains in several location across the area. Stations over southeast PR (the most affected) reported rainfall between 3-6 inches over that weekend. Several flooding and flash flooding events were reported, including one that sadly led to a fatality.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cruz Ortiz Estela Ave. was reported as flooded, near the local Tax Office, Dufresne St., and Luis Munoz Marin Ave. A building was affected.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Flash Flood Watch was issued on the second weekend of November, mainly due to a tropical wave moving through the area and a lingering frontal boundary. Saturated soils and moderate to heavy rainfall helped increase the flood and debris flow threat. This tropical wave brought abundant moisture above typical November levels, along with instability, leading to thunderstorms with frequent lightning, strong, gusty winds, and breezy to windy conditions in parts of the islands. From November 9th to 11th, above 3 inches of rain were reported, across SW and N Puerto Rico, but mainly over SE Puerto Rico and Vieques, (with some stations reporting 5 to 6.69 inches in those areas).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two trapped vehicles were reported in the intersection of PR-31 and PR-53 due to flooding resulted from Rio Blanco.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Under a surface-induced perturbation and associated mid to upper-level through...an unsettled workweek was observed for most of the region. Above normal precipitable water (PWAT) values was observed, with available moisture reaching the mid to upper levels. All of this caused the potential for isolated to scattered thunderstorms to increase. Although PWAT values decreased on Wednesday, fairly unstable conditions, diurnal and local effects promoted continued convective development for the second half of the workweek under ENE winds. This weather pattern lead to quick river rises and water surges with flooding, flash flooding, and landslides.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reported that Rio Cibuco was out of its banks and it |was flooding PR-620 in Sector Fatima. It was also reported that soon could be |flooding PR-647 by Sector Ojo de Agua.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Saturated soils from the previous rainfall events enhanced the risk of flash and river flooding, as another mid to upper-level trough lingered across the region. With increased instability across the Northeast Caribbean, and as a surface high pressure migrated from the western to central Atlantic, moisture was pooled into the region, with total precipitable water values well above the climatological value for these dates. Most of the USGS monitored rivers showed above normal to high levels.|Each day the amount of rainfall was locally significant. In fact, rescues were needed in San Sebasti��n, and Yauco.  The rivers went out of their banks in A��asco, Utuado, Orocovis, Vega Baja, Corozal, Arecibo, San Germ��n, Hormigueros, Mayag��ez, San Juan, Ponce, Ciales, Manat��, and many others without a river gage. At the end of the event, a significant portion of the interior and western Puerto Rico collected over 10���, with isolated amounts surpassing 14. Areas in the east collected more than 5���, all of this in only a couple of days. An astonishing amount of 32 flash flood warnings were issued during this short period of time, with 115 Local Storm Reports created as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Calle Marginal in Rio Grande was flooded due to heavy rains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wet and unstable weather pattern was caused by a surface short-wave trough associated with the remnants of a frontal boundary. The Blended Total Precipitable Water (TPW) Percent of Normal indicated moisture levels exceeding 145% of the climatological normal from the Leeward Islands through the local area. A Saharan Air Layer also helped promote more frequent lightning. Flooding, Landslides (due to high soil saturation) and even a Funnel Cloud were reported under this unsettled weather. By Tuesday (23rd), USGS river sensors still indicate that most rivers over the eastern half of Puerto Rico exceeded the 75th percentile, with half of them surpassing the 90th percentile or well above normal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor Flooding was reported at Esteban B. Cruz St. in Guayama. The road was still passable.

With increased instability across the Northeast Caribbean, and as a surface high pressure migrated from the western to central Atlantic, moisture was pooled into the region, with total precipitable water values well above the climatological value for these dates. Most of the USGS monitored rivers showed above normal to high levels.|Each day the amount of rainfall was locally significant. In fact, rescues were needed in San Sebasti��n, and Yauco.  The rivers went out of their banks in A��asco, Utuado, Orocovis, Vega Baja, Corozal, Arecibo, San Germ��n, Hormigueros, Mayag��ez, San Juan, Ponce, Ciales, Manat��, and many others without a river gage. At the end of the event, a significant portion of the interior and western Puerto Rico collected over 10���, with isolated amounts surpassing 14. Areas in the east collected more than 5���, all of this in only a couple of days. An astonishing amount of 32 flash flood warnings were issued during this short period of time, with 115 Local Storm Reports created as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Calle Santo Domingo near Escuela Jose Onofre in Yauco was affected by flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Under a surface-induced perturbation and associated mid to upper-level through...an unsettled workweek was observed for most of the region. Above normal precipitable water (PWAT) values was observed, with available moisture reaching the mid to upper levels. All of this caused the potential for isolated to scattered thunderstorms to increase. Although PWAT values decreased on Wednesday, fairly unstable conditions, diurnal and local effects promoted continued convective development for the second half of the workweek under ENE winds. This weather pattern lead to quick river rises and water surges with flooding, flash flooding, and landslides.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding was reported at Evaristo Hernandez St. No reported vehicles flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: By early afternoon, the development of more intense convective activity was recorded over southwest PR. Doppler radar estimated between 2 and 4 inches of rain (locally higher) over the municipalities of the SW of PR, where a Flood Advisory was in effect. Additionally, due to isolated to scattered showers the night before, a landslide was recorded in Canovanas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Social media pictures from a TV weather reporter page indicated that Road PR-117 was |flooded, and two creeks were out of their banks. No people trapped.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Increase in moisture, remnants of a cold front to our north and a nearby prefrontal surface trough approached the islands and sinked southward, creating favorable weather |conditions for unsettled weather and above normal moisture. These wet and unstable conditions promoted showery weather for the first half of that week. Flooding, Flash Flooding and Landslides were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash Flooding was reported at PR-185 near La Vega entrance and Rio Canovanas in Canovanas, PR. No people trapped were reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet Station BZDP4 3.6 SE Sumidero reported 2.81 inches of rain at 2:45 PM AST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wet and unstable weather pattern was caused by a surface short-wave trough associated with the remnants of a frontal boundary. The Blended Total Precipitable Water (TPW) Percent of Normal indicated moisture levels exceeding 145% of the climatological normal from the Leeward Islands through the local area. A Saharan Air Layer also helped promote more frequent lightning. Flooding, Landslides (due to high soil saturation) and even a Funnel Cloud were reported under this unsettled weather. By Tuesday (23rd), USGS river sensors still indicate that most rivers over the eastern half of Puerto Rico exceeded the 75th percentile, with half of them surpassing the 90th percentile or well above normal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 911 reporter informed us that a residency in 123 St. in the municipality of Utuado near the McDonalds restaurant, was flooded.

A Saharan Air Layer also helped promote more frequent lightning. Flooding, Landslides (due to high soil saturation) and even a Funnel Cloud were reported under this unsettled weather. By Tuesday (23rd), USGS river sensors still indicate that most rivers over the eastern half of Puerto Rico exceeded the 75th percentile, with half of them surpassing the 90th percentile or well above normal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An impassable road  in PR-5 under the bridge was reported, near the Goya building.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Saturated soils from the previous rainfall events enhanced the risk of flash and river flooding, as another mid to upper-level trough lingered across the region. With increased instability across the Northeast Caribbean, and as a surface high pressure migrated from the western to central Atlantic, moisture was pooled into the region, with total precipitable water values well above the climatological value for these dates. Most of the USGS monitored rivers showed above normal to high levels.|Each day the amount of rainfall was locally significant. In fact, rescues were needed in San Sebasti��n, and Yauco.  The rivers went out of their banks in A��asco, Utuado, Orocovis, Vega Baja, Corozal, Arecibo, San Germ��n, Hormigueros, Mayag��ez, San Juan, Ponce, Ciales, Manat��, and many others without a river gage. At the end of the event, a significant portion of the interior and western Puerto Rico collected over 10���, with isolated amounts surpassing 14. Areas in the east collected more than 5���, all of this in only a couple of days. An astonishing amount of 32 flash flood warnings were issued during this short period of time, with 115 Local Storm Reports created as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: PR-364 was impassable due to a creek out of its banks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Puerto Rico observed two events with above-normal moisture and instability from May 3rd through the 9th. The weather scenario for May 3rd involved an upper-level trough and an induced surface trough with deep moisture from the Caribbean, which enhanced thunderstorms and downpours across most of PR. Besides those atmospheric conditions, it is essential to highlight that April ended up being one of the wettest periods for any other end of the month since we have historical records. The USGS river streamflow stations reported that most of the eastern half of the rivers had streamflows from above-normal to much above-normal ahead of the event. This situation triggered a Flood Watch that was issued for the islands from Friday through Sunday.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports from the 9-1-1 Call Center indicated that a house was being affected by flooding in Calle 31, Arecibo.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Locally generated rip currents on a low-risk day caused a drowning in Isabela.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tourist drowned at Shacks Beach in Isabela after being swept away by rip currents while snorkeling.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On May 2, 2024, a significant weather event unfolded as a waterspout |made landfall, impacting the Cambalache sector within the |Municipality of Arecibo. The inclement weather in this region was |spurred by a mid to upper-level trough shifting from west to east |along Puerto Rico's northern coast. Doppler radar scans revealed |several velocity couplets at an altitude of 6000 feet, indicating |the potential for turbulent weather conditions. As the |waterspout transitioned into a weak tornado, it encountered an |expanse of mangrove trees, resulting in the downing of numerous |small to medium-sized branches along its path. Additionally, the |tornado's winds affected street signs and canopy tents, which were |blown away by the force of the storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At approximately 4:58 PM on May 2, 2024, a waterspout initially |formed over the local Atlantic waters and proceeded to track |ESEward, making landfall over Albacoa Beach in Arecibo. As the |waterspout transitioned into a weak tornado, it encountered an |expanse of mangrove trees, resulting in the downing of numerous |small to medium-sized branches along its path. Additionally, the |tornado's winds affected street signs and canopy tents, which were |blown away by the force of the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Under a surface-induced perturbation and associated mid to upper-level through...an unsettled workweek was observed for most of the region. Above normal precipitable water (PWAT) values was observed, with available moisture reaching the mid to upper levels. All of this caused the potential for isolated to scattered thunderstorms to increase. Although PWAT values decreased on Wednesday, fairly unstable conditions, diurnal and local effects promoted continued convective development for the second half of the workweek under ENE winds. This weather pattern lead to quick river rises and water surges with flooding, flash flooding, and landslides.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency Managers reported Rio Cibuco out of its banks and flooding a bridge (PR-620) near a local church in Sector Fatima, Vega Alta.

With increased instability across the Northeast Caribbean, and as a surface high pressure migrated from the western to central Atlantic, moisture was pooled into the region, with total precipitable water values well above the climatological value for these dates. Most of the USGS monitored rivers showed above normal to high levels.|Each day the amount of rainfall was locally significant. In fact, rescues were needed in San Sebasti��n, and Yauco.  The rivers went out of their banks in A��asco, Utuado, Orocovis, Vega Baja, Corozal, Arecibo, San Germ��n, Hormigueros, Mayag��ez, San Juan, Ponce, Ciales, Manat��, and many others without a river gage. At the end of the event, a significant portion of the interior and western Puerto Rico collected over 10���, with isolated amounts surpassing 14. Areas in the east collected more than 5���, all of this in only a couple of days. An astonishing amount of 32 flash flood warnings were issued during this short period of time, with 115 Local Storm Reports created as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rio de Aibonito went over a bridge on road PR-726, near Edificio Guayacan. The road was impassable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Under a surface-induced perturbation and associated mid to upper-level through...an unsettled workweek was observed for most of the region. Above normal precipitable water (PWAT) values was observed, with available moisture reaching the mid to upper levels. All of this caused the potential for isolated to scattered thunderstorms to increase. Although PWAT values decreased on Wednesday, fairly unstable conditions, diurnal and local effects promoted continued convective development for the second half of the workweek under ENE winds. This weather pattern lead to quick river rises and water surges with flooding, flash flooding, and landslides.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media reported flash flood at PR-30 at Exit 14 from Juncos to Las Piedras.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: By early afternoon, the development of more intense convective activity was recorded over southwest PR. Doppler radar estimated between 2 and 4 inches of rain (locally higher) over the municipalities of the SW of PR, where a Flood Advisory was in effect. Additionally, due to isolated to scattered showers the night before, a landslide was recorded in Canovanas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A landslide was reported at road PR-186, toward Barrio Cubuy. This landslide was likely due to heavy rainfall reported the night before.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southeasterly winds steering warmer tropical air into the islands, and sufficient moisture lead to heat index values exceeding Heat Advisory criteria across most coastal and lower elevation areas. Heat index values exceeded Excessive Heat Warning criteria on up to several days of the second half of May across San Juan and Vicinity, North Central Puerto Rico, Northwestern PR, Mayag��ez and Vicinity and Southwestern PR. Several stations across those areas reported heat indices surpassing 112 degrees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several weather stations reported heat indices above 112 degrees for up to over two hours. These conditions were observed widespread along the coastal areas and the valleys.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Puerto Rico observed two events with above-normal moisture and instability from May 3rd through the 9th. The weather scenario for May 3rd involved an upper-level trough and an induced surface trough with deep moisture from the Caribbean, which enhanced thunderstorms and downpours across most of PR. Besides those atmospheric conditions, it is essential to highlight that April ended up being one of the wettest periods for any other end of the month since we have historical records. The USGS river streamflow stations reported that most of the eastern half of the rivers had streamflows from above-normal to much above-normal ahead of the event. This situation triggered a Flood Watch that was issued for the islands from Friday through Sunday.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was reported along PR-2. Several houses were affected by the flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Under a surface-induced perturbation and associated mid to upper-level through...an unsettled workweek was observed for most of the region. Above normal precipitable water (PWAT) values was observed, with available moisture reaching the mid to upper levels. Although PWAT values decreased on Wednesday, fairly unstable conditions, diurnal and local effects promoted continued convective development for the second half of the workweek under ENE winds. This weather pattern lead to quick river rises and water surges with flooding, flash flooding, and landslides.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reported that several areas in the main town of Naranjito were flooded, close to where the old Williams Pizza was located. This was due to the river being out of its banks.
